journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
title: Predicting rates of manganese oxide reduction from thermodynamic driving forces and structural properties
abstract: Manganese (oxyhydr)oxides are abundant redox-active minerals that influence diverse biogeochemical processes, yet their redox reactivity remains poorly understood due to variations in mineral structure and manganese oxidation state. We quantified the reduction kinetics of three geochemically relevant manganese oxides—birnessite, manganite, and hausmannite—using extracellular electron shuttles with varying redox potentials to systematically modulate the driving force for electron transfer. While the Gibbs free energy (ΔrG) described the kinetics of individual oxides well, the Pourbaix free-energy difference (ΔΨ) offered a distinct advantage by predicting reactivity without requiring detailed knowledge on reaction pathways, making it especially valuable for systems where exact redox reactions are undefined. We further developed a coupled kinetic-mass transport model, which showed that electron-transfer rate constants varied among oxides, whereas mass-transfer coefficients were similar. Classical nucleation theory was applied to contextualize these differences, indicating that the balance between surface and bulk energies controls the dissolution barrier. Our findings not only demonstrate how reaction thermodynamics and phase differences jointly control manganese oxide reduction kinetics but also support a generalizable predictive framework for the reactivity of redox-active minerals across diverse environmental conditions.
discussion: Results and DiscussionKinetics of Manganese Oxide Reduction by EES.To evaluate the effect of mineral properties on the kinetics of manganese oxide reduction, we selected three manganese oxides that differ in manganese oxidation state and crystal structure (Fig. 1A–C): Hausmannite (Mn3O4) is a spinel-type oxide containing both Mn(II) and Mn(III) ; manganite (γ-MnOOH) is a Mn(III) oxide with tunnel structures ; and acid birnessite (K0.27MnO1.95·0.8H2O) is a layered Mn(IV) oxide with structural water and cations in its interlayers . These phases represent the most ubiquitous manganese oxides in soils and sediments, where they often coexist and undergo phase transformations via redox cycles . These oxides undergo reductive dissolution to aqueous Mn2+ according to Eqs. 1–3 : Mn3O4+8H++2e−⇌3Mn2++4H2OEh, H0=1.813V γ-MnOOH+3H++e−⇌Mn2++2H2OEh, M0=1.500V MnO2+4H++2e−⇌Mn2++2H2OEh, AB0=1.290V,Fig. 1.Kinetics of manganese oxide reduction by reduced extracellular electron shuttles. (A–C) Crystal structures of hausmannite, manganite, and acid birnessite (pink = manganese; dark blue = oxygen in MnO6 octahedral sheets; light blue = oxygen of interlayer water; yellow = potassium). Hydrogen atoms are omitted for clarity . The Mn oxidation state distribution was determined by XPS; K content in acid birnessite was quantified by ICP-MS, and H2O content was estimated by mass balance based on the measured Mn and K concentrations, as described in Materials and Methods. (D–F) Surface area-normalized rates (rSA) of manganese reduction by reduced AQDS, CyV, juglone, and riboflavin for the three manganese oxides. Rates were determined for the first 10% of the total reaction, as described in Materials and Methods. Error bars represent the SEM from duplicate measurements.Six panels A to F. Top row A to C show crystal structures. Bottom row D to F show line graphs of r sub S A versus p H from 4 to 10.where Eh0 represents the standard reduction potential and the subscripts H, M, and AB refer to hausmannite, manganite, and acid birnessite, respectively. Given the nonstoichiometric nature of acid birnessite, we performed mediated potentiometry measurements to validate its standard reduction potential . The experimental results showed an Eh, AB0 of 1.304 V (SI Appendix, section S4), confirming that the theoretical value in Eq. 3 is applicable to acid birnessite used in this study .We quantified the kinetics of manganese oxide reduction at various solution pH using the reduced species of the following EES: AQDS, juglone, riboflavin, and CyV . Surface area–normalized manganese reduction rates (rSA) were determined by monitoring changes in the concentration of the reduced EES species using UV–vis spectroscopy (SI Appendix, Figs. S1 and S2). Fig. 1D–F shows that rSA for both hausmannite and manganite increased as pH decreased from 9 to 4.5, whereas acid birnessite exhibited a consistently high rSA of ≈1×105μmol·h−1·m−2. Differences in the pH dependence of rSA between manganese oxides can be attributed to variations in electron–proton coupling associated with their respective reduction half-reaction (Eqs. 1–3). Hausmannite reduction has the highest pH sensitivity, requiring four protons per electron transferred, followed by manganite with three and acid birnessite with two. Hausmannite and manganite exhibited a peak in rSA around pH 8.5 (Fig. 1 D and E) for all EES except CyV. This peak was likely caused by electrostatic attraction between the EES and the manganese oxide surface, as confirmed by surface speciation modeling (SI Appendix, section S7) . The full dataset in Fig. 1D–F revealed that, while pH influenced rSA, EES identity also modulates reduction kinetics, with hausmannite and manganite exhibiting distinct rSA-pH trends depending on the EES used.Relating Manganese Oxide Reduction Rates Across EES to Gibbs Free Energies.To capture the combined influence of pH and EES identity on manganese oxide reduction kinetics, we calculated the Gibbs free energy (ΔrGx0′) for the least exergonic electron transfer step from the reduced EES to the manganese oxide following the approach used by Aeppli et al. for iron oxides . A detailed account of how the ΔrGx0′values were calculated for each manganese oxide-EES pair is provided in SI Appendix, section S8.Fig. 2A–C illustrates the dependence of manganese oxide reduction rates (rSA) on the Gibbs free energy, ΔrGx0′. In alignment with the behavior of iron oxides , the kinetic data correlate most effectively with ΔrGx0′ when it is defined by the least exergonic electron-transfer step of the EES. These results suggest that this least exergonic step acts as a common thermodynamic bottleneck for both manganese and iron oxide reduction. For hausmannite and manganite, rSA generally increased as ΔrGx0′ became more negative (i.e., as the reaction became more thermodynamically favorable) with the exception of rates measured at pH 8.5, as discussed above. rSA values for acid birnessite showed little variation across the investigated ΔrGx0′ range. While plotting rSA as a function of ΔrGx0′ successfully identified the rate-limiting electron transfer step for the EES, the structural transition from manganese oxide to Mn2+ remains less resolved. This is due to the multitude of possible pathways for mineral dissolution, such as the sequential single-electron transfers of acid birnessite (Eqs. 2 and 4) , which are inherently more complex than the one-electron reduction characteristic of iron oxides. MnO2+H++e−⇌MnOOHEh0=1.080VHere, the potential for the first single-electron reduction of MnO2 (Eh0=1.080V) is calculated based on the overall reduction reaction (Eq. 3) and the second electron transfer step (Eq. 2).Fig. 2.Relationships between surface area-normalized rates (rSA) and Gibbs free energies (ΔrGx0′) for manganese oxide reduction by reduced extracellular electron shuttles. Free energies were calculated for the least exergonic electron transfer from reduced EES to (A) hausmannite, (B) manganite, and (C) acid birnessite. x = 1 stands for the one-electron transfer from the semiquinone species of EES to manganese oxides; x = 2 stands for the one-electron transfer from hydroquinone species of EES to manganese oxides. Error bars represent the SE of the mean from duplicate measurements. Kendall’s τB values from rank correlation are reported for the number of data points (n) used in the statistical analysis (τB=0: no correlation, τB=−1: perfect negative correlation).Three panel figure shows log graphs of r sub S A versus delta sub r G sub x super prime for Hausmannite, Manganite, and Birnessite.Relating Manganese Oxide Reduction Rates Across Mineral-EES to Pourbaix Free-Energy Difference.The determination of ΔrGx0′ relies on predefined redox reactions, which may introduce biased thermodynamic estimates in multielectron systems where the full set of possible redox pathways is not known . To address this limitation, we employed the Pourbaix potential (Ψ), which quantifies the absolute free-energy metric of each oxide under given pH and redox conditions, allowing for direct comparison across oxides without requiring reaction-specific assumptions . Ψ is defined as : Ψ=1NMn((G−NKμK−NOμH2O)−RT·ln ·(2NO−NH)pH−(2NO−NH+Q)E),where G is the Gibbs free energy of formation (SI Appendix, Table S3), NMn, NO, and NH are the stoichiometric coefficients of Mn, O, and H atoms in the manganese oxide/ion, respectively, Q is the net charge of the oxide, and E is the redox potential of the environment (i.e., determined by the EES in our case). For acid birnessite, NK is the potassium content in the mineral phase, and the chemical potential of potassium is μK=μK0+RTln[K+], where the activity of K+ is approximated by its aqueous concentration ([K+]).We calculated the Pourbaix free-energy difference (ΔΨ) between the initial manganese oxides hausmannite, manganite, and acid birnessite (Ψsolid) and the aqueous Mn2+ (ΨMn2+) according to: ΔΨ=ΨMnaq2+−ΨsolidFig. 3B shows the relationships between rSA and ΔΨ for the three tested manganese oxides differing in manganese oxidation state and phase structure. Plotting rSA versus ΔΨ shows a similar trend to the ΔrGx0′ analysis, demonstrating that ΔΨ effectively captures the thermodynamic driving force for manganese oxide reduction without relying on the predefined redox pathways. Consequently, in systems where the exact redox reactions are unknown, such as the systems involving structural transition here, ΔΨ is a more effective thermodynamic predictor of manganese oxide reduction kinetics compared to ΔrGx0′.Fig. 3.Relationships between surface area-normalized rates (rSA) and free energies for manganese oxide reduction by reduced extracellular electron shuttles. rSA values are plotted versus (A) Gibbs free energies (ΔrGx0′, replotted from Fig. 2A–C) and (B) Pourbaix free-energy differences (ΔΨ) for hausmannite, manganite, and acid birnessite. Error bars represent the SE of the mean from duplicate measurements.Two panel scatter plot. Panel A and B show r sub S A versus Delta sub r G super 0 prime and Delta Psi.Quantitative Analysis of Regimes Controlling Reduction Rates of Manganese Oxides.The rSA versus ΔΨ relationship in Fig. 3B shows an initial increase in rSA as the reaction conditions became more exergonic (i.e., as ΔΨ became more negative) before plateauing at ≈1×105μmol·h−1·m−2. This plateau occurred at ΔΨ values more negative than ≈−80kJ·mol−1, indicating a shift in the rate-limiting process governing the overall reduction reaction. We attributed the initial exponential increase to a kinetically controlled regime and the subsequent plateau to a mass-transfer-limited process. To capture the interplay between these processes, we developed a model (Materials and Methods) that describes rSA as a function of ΔΨ, using three key parameters: an intrinsic electron-transfer rate constant (k0, s−1), a transfer coefficient (α), and an effective mass-transfer coefficient (kms, m·s−1). These parameters were extracted for each manganese oxide by minimizing the least-squares difference between observed and simulated reduction rates. The resulting fits are shown in Fig. 4, with corresponding parameter values listed in SI Appendix, Table S4.Fig. 4.Quantitative relationships between surface area-normalized rates (rSA) and Pourbaix free-energy differences (ΔΨ) established by kinetic modeling for (A) hausmannite; (B) manganite; and (C) acid birnessite. Error bars represent the SE of the mean from duplicate measurements. Solid lines represent fits based on the kinetic model, and shaded regions indicate the 95% CIs of the simulations.Three line graphs labelled A, B, and C plot r sub S A versus delta psi. Panels A and B show downward curves; panel C shows a horizontal line.The model accurately reproduced the experimental trends for hausmannite (r2=0.91, n=42; Fig. 4A) and reasonably fit the manganite data (r2=0.77, n=28; Fig. 4B). The lower fit quality for manganite is likely due to pH-dependent changes in surface charge—from negative to near neutral—across the experimental range (SI Appendix, Fig. S4), an effect not accounted for in the current model. Notably, restricting the fit to data collected using CyV—a redox mediator minimally affected by surface charge—substantially improved the fit (r2=0.94, n=8; SI Appendix, Fig. S15), suggesting that pH-dependent surface complexation affected the electron-transfer kinetics at the manganite–water interface.Among the manganese oxides tested, hausmannite exhibited the slowest electron-transfer kinetics, with a k0 value of 4.68×10−6 s−1 (SI Appendix, Table S4), approximately three orders of magnitude lower than that of manganite (k0=1.05×10−3 s−1). In contrast, the electron-transfer kinetics of acid birnessite were so rapid that we were unable to distinguish between kinetically limited and mass-transfer-limited regimes, indicating that the reaction was entirely mass-transfer limited under the experimental conditions tested. To rationalize the observed differences in electron-transfer kinetics, we used classical nucleation theory to calculate the nucleation barrier (ΔG∗) for each manganese oxide and examined its relationship with the corresponding rSA values . The nucleation barrier is an activation free energy that reflects the balance between surface and bulk energies of a solid phase (SI Appendix, section S12), and it acts as an energy barrier that must be overcome for the solids to start dissolving . Under the experimental conditions considered, hausmannite consistently exhibited the highest ΔG∗ values across the full range of rSA values, followed by manganite and birnessite (Fig. 5). This ordering is consistent with the trend observed in the intrinsic electron-transfer rate constants, suggesting that electron-transfer kinetics are governed, at least in part, by the nucleation barrier.Fig. 5.Relationship between surface area-normalized rates (rSA) and the nucleation barrier (ΔG∗) for hausmannite, manganite, and birnessite. The nucleation barrier was calculated as described in SI Appendix, section S12. Error bars represent the SE of the mean from duplicate measurements. The Kendall’s τB value from rank correlation is reported for n=86 data points used in the statistical analysis (τB=0: no correlation, τB=−1: perfect negative correlation).Scatter plot of r sub S A versus delta G super asterisk showing a negative correlation for Hausmannite, Manganite, and Birnessite.In contrast to the k0 values, all three manganese oxides exhibited similar mass-transfer coefficients (kmt), ranging from 0.50 to 1.58×10−4 m·s−1 (SI Appendix, Table S4). This similarity suggests that under highly exergonic conditions (i.e., large negative ΔΨ values), the rate-determining step was effectively the same across all three systems. To assess the plausibility of the fitted kmt values, we estimated the diffusion layer thickness using representative EES diffusion coefficients from the literature, which yielded a value of ≈5μm (SI Appendix, section S11), which is consistent with expectations for particles suspended in a turbulent environment . Together, these results show that while mass transfer governs reactivity under highly exergonic conditions, intrinsic electron-transfer kinetics—influenced by the nucleation barrier of the manganese oxide—control rates at less exergonic conditions.Implications.This study advances our understanding of the kinetics of manganese oxide reduction by integrating thermodynamic and structural factors into a unified predictive framework. Using the Pourbaix potential as a thermodynamic descriptor, we accounted for phase stability, pH, and redox conditions, enabling more accurate predictions of reduction rates across three structurally distinct manganese oxides. Additionally, our coupled kinetic-mass transport model separates intrinsic electron transfer rates from mass transport limitations, offering clearer insights into the processes controlling manganese oxide reduction under varying thermodynamic conditions.Although our study focuses on manganese oxides, our approach has broad applicability to a variety of redox-active minerals and complex geochemical environments. The ability to predict mineral reactivity without requiring detailed reaction stoichiometry is especially useful in natural systems where chemically diverse redox species coexist. The use of Pourbaix potentials as thermodynamic predictors could significantly enhance our capacity to model and predict environmentally relevant geochemical processes. While we deliberately isolate thermodynamic controls on intrinsic reduction kinetics in this study, natural systems often involve reductant and Mn2+ sorption onto oxide surfaces, which can influence reactive-site availability and thus apparent rates and longer-term redox behavior. Future work could extend the framework herein to other redox-active mineral systems and explore the influence of particle size and surface energy on reactivity, as the Pourbaix potential offers a seamless way to incorporate surface energy effects when determining thermodynamic driving forces for a reaction.
